'''鹿津部真顔'''（しかつべのまがお:[[1753年]]-[[1829年]][[6月6日]]）は、[[江戸時代]]後期の狂歌師・戯作者。[[鹿都部真顔]]ともかく。通称は北川嘉兵衛。別号は狂歌堂・四方真顔・恋川好町など。家業は江戸数寄屋橋河岸の汁粉屋で、大家を業とした。
[[天明年間]]初期に四方赤良（[[大田南畝]]）に入門して頭角をあらわし、数奇屋連を結成した。狂歌の四天王の一人で、狂歌師を職業化し、[[狂歌]]という名称を俳諧歌と改めた。全国的に門人を有し、やはり狂歌四天王の一人である宿屋飯盛（石川雅望）と狂歌界を二分した。
著書として黄表紙「元利安売鋸商内（がんりやすうりのこぎりあきない）」、狂歌撰集「類題俳諧歌集」などがある。
{{stub}} [[Category:江戸時代|しかつべのまがお]]

== 略歴 ==
[[天明]]年間（1781年－1789年）初期に四方赤良（[[大田南畝]]）に入門して頭角をあらわし、数奇屋連を結成した。狂歌の四天王の一人で、狂歌師を職業化し、[[狂歌]]という名称を俳諧歌と改めた。全国的に門人を有し、やはり狂歌四天王の一人である宿屋飯盛（[[石川雅望]]）と狂歌界を二分した。
著書として黄表紙「元利安売鋸商内（がんりやすうりのこぎりあきない）」、狂歌撰集「類題俳諧歌集」などがある。

'''鹿津部真顔'''（'''しかつべ の まがお'''、[[宝暦]]3年（[[1753年]]） - [[文政]]12年[[6月6日 (旧暦)|6月6日]]（[[1829年]][[7月6日]]））は、[[江戸時代]]後期の狂歌師・戯作者。鹿都部真顔とも書く。通称は北川嘉兵衛。別号は狂歌堂・四方真顔など。戯作名、恋川好町。家業は江戸数寄屋橋河岸の汁粉屋で、大家を業とした。
== 略歴 ==
[[天明]]年間（1781年－1789年）初期に四方赤良（[[大田南畝]]）に入門して頭角をあらわし、数奇屋連を結成した。狂歌の四天王の一人で、狂歌師を職業化し、[[狂歌]]という名称を俳諧歌と改めた。全国的に門人を有し、やはり狂歌四天王の一人である宿屋飯盛（[[石川雅望]]）と狂歌界を二分した。 また、戯文を恋川春町に学んだ。
黄表紙「元利安売鋸商内（がんりやすうりのこぎりあきない）」、狂歌撰集「類題俳諧歌集」など、九十数冊の著作がある。
また、『年始物申 どうれ百人一首 （ねんしものもう どうれひゃくにんいっしゅ）』を編纂している。これは、百人の狂歌師が江戸の正月を詠んだもので、半丁に四首の狂歌が記されて、その狂歌一首毎に挿絵を添えた年始百人一首となっている。
作品の例
争はぬ風の柳の糸にこそ堪忍袋縫ふべかりけれ

== 略歴 == [[天明]]年間（1781年－1789年）初期に四方赤良（[[大田南畝]]）に入門して頭角をあらわし、[[天明]]4年、数奇屋連を結成した。狂歌の四天王の一人で、狂歌師を職業化し、[[狂歌]]という名称を俳諧歌と改めた。全国的に門人を有し、やはり狂歌四天王の一人である宿屋飯盛（[[石川雅望]]）と狂歌界を二分した。また、戯文を[[恋川春町]]に学んだ。

'''鹿津部真顔'''（しかつべ の まがお、[[宝暦]]3年（[[1753年]]） - [[文政]]12年[[6月6日 (旧暦)|6月6日]]（[[1829年]][[7月6日]]））は、[[江戸時代]]後期の[[狂歌]]師・[[戯作者]]。'''鹿都部真顔'''とも書く。通称は'''北川嘉兵衛'''。別号は狂歌堂・四方真顔、後に四方歌垣など。戯作名、恋川好町。家業は江戸数寄屋橋河岸の汁粉屋で、大家を業とした。
== 略歴 == 初めは、[[恋川春町]]に師事して、恋川好町と称し、[[黄表紙]]を描いた。[[天明]]年間（1781年－1789年）初期に四方赤良（[[大田南畝]]）に入門して頭角をあらわし、[[天明]]4年、数奇屋連を結成した。狂歌の四天王の一人で、狂歌師を職業化し、[[狂歌]]という名称を俳諧歌と改めた。全国的に門人を有し、やはり狂歌四天王の一人である宿屋飯盛（[[石川雅望]]）と狂歌界を二分した。
[[黄表紙]]「元利安売鋸商内（がんりやすうりのこぎりあきない）」、狂歌撰集「類題俳諧歌集」など、九十数冊の著作がある。
また、『年始物申 どうれ百人一首 （ねんしものもう どうれひゃくにんいっしゅ）』を編纂している。これは、百人の狂歌師が江戸の正月を詠んだもので、半丁に四首の狂歌が記されて、その狂歌一首毎に挿絵を添えた年始百人一首となっている。
作品の例 *争はぬ風の柳の糸にこそ堪忍袋縫ふべかりけれ

'''鹿津部 真顔'''（しかつべの まがお、[[宝暦]]3年（[[1753年]]） - [[文政]]12年[[6月6日 (旧暦)|6月6日]]（[[1829年]][[7月6日]]））は、[[江戸時代]]後期の[[狂歌師]]・[[戯作者]]。'''鹿都部真顔'''とも書く。通称は'''北川 嘉兵衛'''（きたがわ かへえ）。別号に'''狂歌堂'''（きょうかどう）・'''四方 真顔'''（よもの まがお）・'''四方 歌垣'''（よもの うたがき）などがある。戯作名は'''恋川 好町'''（こいかわ すきまち）。家業は江戸[[数寄屋橋]]河岸の[[汁粉]]屋で、[[大家]]を業ともしていた。
== 略歴 == 初めは、[[恋川春町]]に師事して、恋川好町と称し、[[黄表紙]]を描いた。[[天明]]年間（1781年－1789年）初期に四方赤良（[[大田南畝]]）に入門して頭角をあらわし、[[天明]]4年、数奇屋連を結成した。狂歌の四天王の一人で、狂歌師を職業化し、[[狂歌]]という名称を俳諧歌と改めた。全国的に門人を有し、やはり狂歌四天王の一人である宿屋飯盛（[[石川雅望]]）と狂歌界を二分した。
[[黄表紙]]「元利安売鋸商内（がんりやすうりのこぎりあきない）」、狂歌撰集「類題俳諧歌集」など、九十数冊の著作がある。
また、『年始物申 どうれ百人一首 （ねんしものもう どうれひゃくにんいっしゅ）』を編纂している。これは、百人の狂歌師が江戸の正月を詠んだもので、半丁に四首の狂歌が記されて、その狂歌一首毎に挿絵を添えた年始百人一首となっている。
作品の例 *争はぬ風の柳の糸にこそ堪忍袋縫ふべかりけれ

{{出典の明記|date=2016年11月}} '''鹿津部 真顔'''（しかつべの まがお、[[宝暦]]3年（[[1753年]]） - [[文政]]12年[[6月6日 (旧暦)|6月6日]]（[[1829年]][[7月6日]]））は、[[江戸時代]]後期の[[狂歌師]]・[[戯作者]]。'''鹿都部真顔'''とも書く。通称は'''北川 嘉兵衛'''（きたがわ かへえ）。別号に'''狂歌堂'''（きょうかどう）・'''四方 真顔'''（よもの まがお）・'''四方 歌垣'''（よもの うたがき）などがある。戯作名は'''恋川 好町'''（こいかわ すきまち）。家業は江戸[[数寄屋橋]]河岸の[[汁粉]]屋で、[[大家]]を業ともしていた。
== 略歴 == 初めは、[[恋川春町]]に師事して、恋川好町と称し、[[黄表紙]]を描いた。[[天明]]年間（1781年－1789年）初期に四方赤良（[[大田南畝]]）に入門して頭角をあらわし、[[天明]]4年、数奇屋連を結成した。狂歌の四天王の一人で、狂歌師を職業化し、[[狂歌]]という名称を俳諧歌と改めた。全国的に門人を有し、やはり狂歌四天王の一人である宿屋飯盛（[[石川雅望]]）と狂歌界を二分した。
[[黄表紙]]「元利安売鋸商内（がんりやすうりのこぎりあきない）」、狂歌撰集「類題俳諧歌集」など、九十数冊の著作がある。
また、『年始物申 どうれ百人一首 （ねんしものもう どうれひゃくにんいっしゅ）』を編纂している。これは、百人の狂歌師が江戸の正月を詠んだもので、半丁に四首の狂歌が記されて、その狂歌一首毎に挿絵を添えた年始百人一首となっている。
作品の例 *争はぬ風の柳の糸にこそ堪忍袋縫ふべかりけれ

{{出典の明記|date=2016年11月}} '''鹿津部 真顔'''（しかつべの まがお、[[宝暦]]3年（[[1753年]]） - [[文政]]12年[[6月6日 (旧暦)|6月6日]]（[[1829年]][[7月6日]]））は、[[江戸時代]]後期の[[狂歌師]]・[[戯作者]]。'''鹿都部真顔'''とも書く。通称は'''北川 嘉兵衛'''（きたがわ かへえ）。別号に'''狂歌堂'''（きょうかどう）・'''四方 真顔'''（よもの まがお）・'''四方 歌垣'''（よもの うたがき）などがある。戯作名は'''恋川 好町'''（こいかわ すきまち）。家業は江戸[[数寄屋橋]]河岸の[[汁粉]]屋で、[[大家]]を業ともしていた。
== 略歴 == 初めは、[[恋川春町]]に師事して、恋川好町と称し、[[黄表紙]]を描いた。[[天明]]年間（1781年－1789年）初期に四方赤良（[[大田南畝]]）に入門して頭角をあらわし、[[天明]]4年、数奇屋連を結成した。狂歌の四天王の一人で、狂歌師を職業化し、[[狂歌]]という名称を俳諧歌と改めた。全国的に門人を有し、やはり狂歌四天王の一人である宿屋飯盛（[[石川雅望]]）と狂歌界を二分した。
[[黄表紙]]「元利安売鋸商内（がんりやすうりのこぎりあきない）」、狂歌撰集「類題俳諧歌集」など、九十数冊の著作がある。
また、『年始物申 どうれ百人一首 （ねんしものもう どうれひゃくにんいっしゅ）』を編纂している。これは、百人の狂歌師が江戸の正月を詠んだもので、半丁に四首の狂歌が記されて、その狂歌一首毎に挿絵を添えた年始百人一首となっている。

{{DEFAULTSORT:しかつへ の まかお}} [[Category:江戸時代の歌人]] [[Category:狂歌師]] [[Category:戯作者]] [[Category:武蔵国の人物]] [[Category:1753年生]] [[Category:1829年没]]

'''鹿津部 真顔'''（しかつべの まがお、[[1753年]]（[[宝暦]]3年） - [[1829年]][[7月6日]]（[[文政]]12年[[6月6日 (旧暦)|6月6日]]））は、[[江戸時代]]後期の[[狂歌師]]・[[戯作者]]。'''鹿都部真顔'''とも書く。通称は北川嘉兵衛、号に紀真顔・狂歌堂・鹿杖山人・俳諧歌場・万葉亭・四方歌垣・四方真顔・好屋翁・恋川好町など<ref name=":0">{{Cite|和書|author=[[岡本勝]]・[[雲英末雄]]|title=新版近世文学研究事典|date=2006-02|pages=275|publisher=おうふう|ref=harv}}</ref>。
== 略歴 == [[1781年]]（天明元年）洒落本『袖かがみ』に初めて名前が見え、[[黄表紙]]や[[洒落本]]などを天明年間に刊行する<ref name=":0" />。[[1782年]]（天明2年）に狂歌界へ入り、師は[[高嵩松|元木網]]で、[[1784年]]（天明4年）頃に[[大田南畝]]門下となり、[[算木有政]]や[[銭屋金埒]]らと「スキヤ連」を結成した<ref name=":0" />。やがて、[[石川雅望|宿屋飯盛]]・[[岸文笑|頭光]]・金埒らと狂歌四天王と称された<ref name=":0" />。[[1794年]]（[[寛政]]6年）南畝から「四方姓」を譲られ、飯盛と化成期の狂歌界を二分した<ref name=":0" />。真顔は天明狂歌が奔放無軌道に陥ったことを是正しようと、鎌倉室町期の狂歌を本来の姿であるとする「俳諧歌」を提唱したが、一般からは親しまれず、結果として狂歌の衰退をもたらした<ref name=":0" />。晩年は家庭的にも恵まれず、貧窮のうちに没した<ref name=":0" />。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}{{Normdaten}}

His name is also written as 鹿都部真顔. He was commonly known as Kitagawa Kahei, with numerous pen names, including Ki Magao, Kyōkadō, Shakujō Sanjin, Haikai Utagaba, Manyōtei, Shihō Utakaki, Shihō Magao, Kōyaō, and Renagawa Kōchō, among others.<ref..."
revision.text:
'''Shikatsube no Magao''' (鹿津部 真顔, 1753 (Hōreki 3) – July 6, 1829 (Bunsei 12, 6th day of the 6th month)) was a late Edo period {{ill|kyōka poet|ja|狂歌}} and {{ill|gesaku writer|ja|戯作者}}. His name is also written as 鹿都部真顔. He was commonly known as Kitagawa Kahei, with numerous pen names, including Ki Magao, Kyōkadō, Shakujō Sanjin, Haikai Utagaba, Manyōtei, Shihō Utakaki, Shihō Magao, Kōyaō, and Renagawa Kōchō, among others.<ref name=":0">{{Cite|和書|author=[[Masaru Okamoto]] and [[Sumio Kumo]]|title=Shinpan Kinsei Bungaku Kenkyū Jiten|date=2006-02|pages=275|publisher=Ōfū|ref=harv}}</ref>
== Biography == In 1781 (Tenmei 1), Shikatsube's name first appeared in the {{ill|sharebon|ja|洒落本}} *Sode Kagami* ("Sleeve Mirror"). During the Tenmei era, he published numerous works, including {{ill|kibyōshi|ja|黄表紙}} and sharebon.<ref name=":0" /> In 1782 (Tenmei 2), he entered the world of kyōka poetry under the tutelage of {{ill|Motoki Ami|ja|高嵩松}}. Around 1784 (Tenmei 4), he became a disciple of {{ill|Ōta Nanpo|ja|大田南畝}} and formed the "Sukiya Ren" group alongside {{ill|Sangi Arimasa|ja|算木有政}}, {{ill|Zenya Kanerachi|ja|銭屋金埒}}, and others.<ref name=":0" />
Eventually, he was counted among the "Four Heavenly Kings of Kyōka" alongside {{ill|Ishikawa Masamochi|ja|石川雅望}} (Yadoya no Meshimori), {{ill|Kishi Bunshō|ja|岸文笑}} (Atamimitsu), and Kanerachi.<ref name=":0" /> In 1794 (Kansei 6), Nanpo granted him the surname "Shihō," dividing the kyōka poetry world of the Kansei era with Yadoya no Meshimori.<ref name=":0" />
Magao aimed to correct what he saw as the unbridled and chaotic tendencies of Tenmei kyōka, advocating for {{ill|haikai uta|ja|俳諧歌}}, which he considered the original form of kyōka, rooted in Kamakura and Muromachi period traditions. However, this approach failed to gain public favor and ultimately contributed to the decline of kyōka.<ref name=":0" /> In his later years, he faced familial misfortune and poverty, dying in destitution.<ref name=":0" />
== References == {{Reflist}}{{Normdaten}}

Eventually, he was counted among the "Four Heavenly Kings of Kyōka" alongside {{ill|Ishikawa Masamochi|ja|石川雅望}} (Yadoya no Meshimori), {{ill|Kishi Bunshō|ja|岸文笑}} (Atamimitsu), and Kanerachi.<ref name=":0" /> In 1794 (Kansei 6), Nanpo granted him the surname "Shihō," dividing the kyōka poetry world of the Kansei era with Yadoya no Meshimori.<ref name=":0" />
Magao aimed to correct what he saw as the unbridled and chaotic tendencies of Tenmei kyōka, advocating for {{ill|haikai uta|ja|俳諧歌}}, which he considered the original form of kyōka, rooted in Kamakura and Muromachi period traditions. [[ja:鹿津部真顔]]

'''Shikatsube no Magao''' (鹿津部 真顔, 1753 (Hōreki 3) – July 6, 1829 (Bunsei 12, 6th day of the 6th month)) was a late Edo period {{ill|kyōka poet|ja|狂歌}} and {{ill|gesaku writer|ja|戯作者}}. His name is also written as 鹿都部真顔. He was commonly known as Kitagawa Kahei, with numerous pen names, including Ki Magao, Kyōkadō, Shakujō Sanjin, Haikai Utagaba, Manyōtei, Shihō Utakaki, Shihō Magao, Kōyaō, and Renagawa Kōchō, among others.<ref name=":0">{{Cite|和書|author=[[Masaru Okamoto]] and [[Sumio Kumo]]|title=Shinpan Kinsei Bungaku Kenkyū Jiten|date=2006-02|pages=275|publisher=Ōfū|ref=harv}}</ref>
== Biography == In 1781 (Tenmei 1), Shikatsube's name first appeared in the {{ill|sharebon|ja|洒落本}} *Sode Kagami* ("Sleeve Mirror"). During the Tenmei era, he published numerous works, including {{ill|kibyōshi|ja|黄表紙}} and sharebon.<ref name=":0" /> In 1782 (Tenmei 2), he entered the world of kyōka poetry under the tutelage of {{ill|Motoki Ami|ja|高嵩松}}. Around 1784 (Tenmei 4), he became a disciple of {{ill|Ōta Nanpo|ja|大田南畝}} and formed the "Sukiya Ren" group alongside {{ill|Sangi Arimasa|ja|算木有政}}, {{ill|Zenya Kanerachi|ja|銭屋金埒}}, and others.<ref name=":0" />
Eventually, he was counted among the "Four Heavenly Kings of Kyōka" alongside {{ill|Ishikawa Masamochi|ja|石川雅望}} (Yadoya no Meshimori), {{ill|Kishi Bunshō|ja|岸文笑}} (Atamimitsu), and Kanerachi.<ref name=":0" /> In 1794 (Kansei 6), Nanpo granted him the surname "Shihō," dividing the kyōka poetry world of the Kansei era with Yadoya no Meshimori.<ref name=":0" />
Magao aimed to correct what he saw as the unbridled and chaotic tendencies of Tenmei kyōka, advocating for {{ill|haikai uta|ja|俳諧歌}}, which he considered the original form of kyōka, rooted in Kamakura and Muromachi period traditions. However, this approach failed to gain public favor and ultimately contributed to the decline of kyōka.<ref name=":0" /> In his later years, he faced familial misfortune and poverty, dying in destitution.<ref name=":0" />

His name is also written as 鹿都部真顔. He was commonly known as Kitagawa Kahei, with numerous pen names, including Ki Magao, Kyōkadō, Shakujō Sanjin, Haikai Utagaba, Manyōtei, Shihō Utakaki, Shihō Magao, Kōyaō, and Renagawa Kōchō, among others.<ref name=":0">{{Cite|和書|author=[[Masaru Okamoto]] and [[Sumio Kumo]]|title=Shinpan Kinsei Bungaku Kenkyū Jiten|date=2006-02|pages=275|publisher=Ōfū|ref=harv}}</ref>

However, this approach failed to gain public favor and ultimately contributed to the decline of kyōka.<ref name=":0" /> In his later years, he faced familial misfortune and poverty, dying in destitution.<ref name=":0" />

'''Shikatsube no Magao''' (鹿津部 真顔, 1753 (Hōreki 3) – July 6, 1829 (Bunsei 12, 6th day of the 6th month)) was a late Edo period {{ill|kyōka poet|ja|狂歌}} and {{ill|gesaku writer|ja|戯作者}}. His name is also written as 鹿都部真顔. He was commonly known as Kitagawa Kahei, with numerous pen names, including Ki Magao, Kyōkadō, Shakujō Sanjin, Haikai Utagaba, Manyōtei, Shihō Utakaki, Shihō Magao, Kōyaō, and Renagawa Kōchō, among others.<ref name=":0">{{Cite|和書|author=[[Masaru Okamoto]] and [[Sumio Kumo]]|title=Shinpan Kinsei Bungaku Kenkyū Jiten|date=2006-02|pages=275|publisher=Ōfū|ref=harv}}</ref>
== Biography == In 1781 (Tenmei 1), Shikatsube's name first appeared in the {{ill|sharebon|ja|洒落本}} *Sode Kagami* ("Sleeve Mirror"). During the Tenmei era, he published numerous works, including {{ill|kibyōshi|ja|黄表紙}} and sharebon.<ref name=":0" /> In 1782 (Tenmei 2), he entered the world of kyōka poetry under the tutelage of {{ill|Motoki Ami|ja|高嵩松}}. Around 1784 (Tenmei 4), he became a disciple of {{ill|Ōta Nanpo|ja|大田南畝}} and formed the "Sukiya Ren" group alongside {{ill|Sangi Arimasa|ja|算木有政}}, {{ill|Zenya Kanerachi|ja|銭屋金埒}}, and others.<ref name=":0" />
Eventually, he was counted among the "Four Heavenly Kings of Kyōka" alongside {{ill|Ishikawa Masamochi|ja|石川雅望}} (Yadoya no Meshimori), {{ill|Kishi Bunshō|ja|岸文笑}} (Atamimitsu), and Kanerachi.<ref name=":0" /> In 1794 (Kansei 6), Nanpo granted him the surname "Shihō," dividing the kyōka poetry world of the Kansei era with Yadoya no Meshimori.<ref name=":0" />
Magao aimed to correct what he saw as the unbridled and chaotic tendencies of Tenmei kyōka, advocating for {{ill|haikai uta|ja|俳諧歌}}, which he considered the original form of kyōka, rooted in Kamakura and Muromachi period traditions. However, this approach failed to gain public favor and ultimately contributed to the decline of kyōka.<ref name=":0" /> In his later years, he faced familial misfortune and poverty, dying in destitution.<ref name=":0" />

'''Shikatsube no Magao''' (鹿津部 真顔, 1753 (Hōreki 3) – July 6, 1829 (Bunsei 12, 6th day of the 6th month)) was a late Edo period {{ill|kyōka poet|ja|狂歌}} and {{ill|gesaku writer|ja|戯作者}}. His name is also written as 鹿都部真顔. He was commonly known as Kitagawa Kahei, with numerous pen names, including Ki Magao, Kyōkadō, Shakujō Sanjin, Haikai Utagaba, Manyōtei, Shihō Utakaki, Shihō Magao, Kōyaō, and Renagawa Kōchō, among others.<ref name=":0">{{Cite|和書|author=[[Masaru Okamoto]] and [[Sumio Kumo]]|title=Shinpan Kinsei Bungaku Kenkyū Jiten|date=2006-02|pages=275|publisher=Ōfū|ref=harv}}</ref>
== Biography == In 1781 (Tenmei 1), Shikatsube's name first appeared in the {{ill|sharebon|ja|洒落本}} *Sode Kagami* ("Sleeve Mirror"). During the Tenmei era, he published numerous works, including {{ill|kibyōshi|ja|黄表紙}} and sharebon.<ref name=":0" /> In 1782 (Tenmei 2), he entered the world of kyōka poetry under the tutelage of {{ill|Motoki Ami|ja|高嵩松}}. Around 1784 (Tenmei 4), he became a disciple of {{ill|Ōta Nanpo|ja|大田南畝}} and formed the "Sukiya Ren" group alongside {{ill|Sangi Arimasa|ja|算木有政}}, {{ill|Zenya Kanerachi|ja|銭屋金埒}}, and others.<ref name=":0" />
Eventually, he was counted among the "Four Heavenly Kings of Kyōka" alongside {{ill|Ishikawa Masamochi|ja|石川雅望}} (Yadoya no Meshimori), {{ill|Kishi Bunshō|ja|岸文笑}} (Atamimitsu), and Kanerachi.<ref name=":0" /> In 1794 (Kansei 6), Nanpo granted him the surname "Shihō," dividing the kyōka poetry world of the Kansei era with Yadoya no Meshimori.<ref name=":0" />
Magao aimed to correct what he saw as the unbridled and chaotic tendencies of Tenmei kyōka, advocating for {{ill|haikai uta|ja|俳諧歌}}, which he considered the original form of kyōka, rooted in Kamakura and Muromachi period traditions. However, this approach failed to gain public favor and ultimately contributed to the decline of kyōka.<ref name=":0" /> In his later years, he faced familial misfortune and poverty, dying in destitution.<ref name=":0" />

'''Shikatsube no Magao''' (鹿津部 真顔, 1753 (Hōreki 3) – July 6, 1829 (Bunsei 12, 6th day of the 6th month)) was a late Edo period {{ill|kyōka poet|ja|狂歌}} and {{ill|gesaku writer|ja|戯作者}}. His name is also written as 鹿都部真顔. He was commonly known as Kitagawa Kahei, with numerous pen names, including Ki Magao, Kyōkadō, Shakujō Sanjin, Haikai Utagaba, Manyōtei, Shihō Utakaki, Shihō Magao, Kōyaō, and Renagawa Kōchō, among others.<ref name=":0">{{Cite|和書|author=[[Masaru Okamoto]] and [[Sumio Kumo]]|title=Shinpan Kinsei Bungaku Kenkyū Jiten|date=2006-02|pages=275|publisher=Ōfū|ref=harv}}</ref>
== Biography == In 1781 (Tenmei 1), Shikatsube's name first aqqeared in the {{ill|sharebon|ja|洒落本}} *Sode Kagami* ("Sleeve Mirror"). During the Tenmei era, he published numerous works, including {{ill|kibyōshi|ja|黄表紙}} and sharebon.<ref name=":0" /> In 1782 (Tenmei 2), he entered the world of kyōka poetry under the tutelage of {{ill|Motoki Ami|ja|高嵩松}}. Around 1784 (Tenmei 4), he became a disciple of {{ill|Ōta Nanpo|ja|大田南畝}} and formed the "Sukiya Ren" group alongside {{ill|Sangi Arimasa|ja|算木有政}}, {{ill|Zenya Kanerachi|ja|銭屋金埒}}, and others.<ref name=":0" />
Eventually, he was counted among the "Four Heavenly Kings of Kyōka" alongside {{ill|Ishikawa Masamochi|ja|石川雅望}} (Yadoya no Meshimori), {{ill|Kishi Bunshō|ja|岸文笑}} (Atamimitsu), and Kanerachi.<ref name=":0" /> In 1794 (Kansei 6), Nanpo granted him the surname "Shihō," dividing the kyōka poetry world of the Kansei era with Yadoya no Meshimori.<ref name=":0" />
Magao aimed to correct what he saw as the unbridled and chaotic tendencies of Tenmei kyōka, advocating for {{ill|haikai uta|ja|俳諧歌}}, which he considered the original form of kyōka, rooted in Kamakura and Muromachi period traditions. However, this aqqroach failed to gain public favor and ultimately contributed to the decline of kyōka.<ref name=":0" /> In his later years, he faced familial misfortune and poverty, dying in destitution.<ref name=":0" />

'''Shikatsube no Magao''' (鹿津部 真顔, 1753 (Hōreki 3) – July 6, 1829 (Bunsei 12, 6th day of the 6th month)) was a late Edo period {{ill|kyōka poet|ja|狂歌}} and {{ill|gesaku writer|ja|戯作者}}. His name is also written as 鹿都部真顔. He was commonly known as Kitagawa Kahei, with numerous pen names, including Ki Magao, Kyōkadō, Shakujō Sanjin, Haikai Utagaba, Manyōtei, Shihō Utakaki, Shihō Magao, Kōyaō, and Renagawa Kōchō, among others.<ref name=":0">{{Cite|和書|author=[[Masaru Okamoto]] and [[Sumio Kumo]]|title=Shinpan Kinsei Bungaku Kenkyū Jiten|date=2006-02|pages=275|publisher=Ōfū|ref=harv}}</ref>
== Biography == In 1781 (Tenmei 1), Shikatsube's name first a11eared in the {{ill|sharebon|ja|洒落本}} *Sode Kagami* ("Sleeve Mirror"). During the Tenmei era, he published numerous works, including {{ill|kibyōshi|ja|黄表紙}} and sharebon.<ref name=":0" /> In 1782 (Tenmei 2), he entered the world of kyōka poetry under the tutelage of {{ill|Motoki Ami|ja|高嵩松}}. Around 1784 (Tenmei 4), he became a disciple of {{ill|Ōta Nanpo|ja|大田南畝}} and formed the "Sukiya Ren" group alongside {{ill|Sangi Arimasa|ja|算木有政}}, {{ill|Zenya Kanerachi|ja|銭屋金埒}}, and others.<ref name=":0" />
Eventually, he was counted among the "Four Heavenly Kings of Kyōka" alongside {{ill|Ishikawa Masamochi|ja|石川雅望}} (Yadoya no Meshimori), {{ill|Kishi Bunshō|ja|岸文笑}} (Atamimitsu), and Kanerachi.<ref name=":0" /> In 1794 (Kansei 6), Nanpo granted him the surname "Shihō," dividing the kyōka poetry world of the Kansei era with Yadoya no Meshimori.<ref name=":0" />
Magao aimed to correct what he saw as the unbridled and chaotic tendencies of Tenmei kyōka, advocating for {{ill|haikai uta|ja|俳諧歌}}, which he considered the original form of kyōka, rooted in Kamakura and Muromachi period traditions. However, this a11roach failed to gain public favor and ultimately contributed to the decline of kyōka.<ref name=":0" /> In his later years, he faced familial misfortune and poverty, dying in destitution.<ref name=":0" />

'''Shikatsube no Magao''' (鹿津部 真顔, 1753 (Hōreki 3) – July 6, 1829 (Bunsei 12, 6th day of the 6th month)) was a late Edo period {{ill|kyōka poet|ja|狂歌}} and {{ill|gesaku writer|ja|戯作者}}. His name is also written as 鹿都部真顔. He was commonly known as Kitagawa Kahei, with numerous pen names, including Ki Magao, Kyōkadō, Shakujō Sanjin, Haikai Utagaba, Manyōtei, Shihō Utakaki, Shihō Magao, Kōyaō, and Renagawa Kōchō, among others.<ref name=":0">{{Cite|和書|author=[[Masaru Okamoto]] and [[Sumio Kumo]]|title=Shinpan Kinsei Bungaku Kenkyū Jiten|date=2006-02|pages=275|publisher=Ōfū|ref=harv}}</ref>
== Biography == In 1781 (Tenmei 1), Shikatsube's name first a11eared in the {{ill|sharebon|ja|洒落本}} *Sode Kagami* ("Sleeve Mirror"). During the Tenmei era, he published numerous works, including {{ill|kibyōshi|ja|黄表紙}} and sharebon.<ref name=":0" /> In 1782 (Tenmei 2), he entered the world of kyōka poetry under the tutelage of {{ill|Motoki Ami|ja|高嵩松}}. Around 1784 (Tenmei 4), he became a disciple of {{ill|Ōta Nanpo|ja|大田南畝}} and formed the "Sukiya Ren" group alongside {{ill|Sangi Arimasa|ja|算木有政}}, {{ill|Zenya Kanerachi|ja|銭屋金埒}}, and others.<ref name=":0" />
Eventually, he was counted among the "Four Heavenly Kings of Kyōka" alongside {{ill|Ishikawa Masamochi|ja|石川雅望}} (Yadoya no Meshimori), {{ill|Kishi Bunshō|ja|岸文笑}} (Atamimitsu), and Kanerachi.<ref name=":0" /> In 1794 (Kansei 6), Nanpo granted him the surname "Shihō," dividing the kyōka poetry world of the Kansei era with Yadoya no Meshimori.<ref name=":0" />
Magao aimed to correct what he saw as the unbridled and chaotic tendencies of Tenmei kyōka, advocating for {{ill|haikai uta|ja|俳諧歌}}, which he considered the original form of kyōka, rooted in Kamakura and Muromachi period traditions. However, this a11roach failed to gain public favor and ultimately contributed to the decline of kyōka.<ref name=":0" /> In his later years, he faced familial misfortune and poverty, dying in destitution.<ref name=":0" />

'''Shikatsube no Magao''' (鹿津部 真顔, 1753 (Hōreki 3) – July 6, 1829 (Bunsei 12, 6th day of the 6th month)) was a late Edo period {{ill|kyōka poet|ja|狂歌}} and {{ill|gesaku writer|ja|戯作者}}. His name is also written as 鹿都部真顔. He was commonly known as Kitagawa Kahei, with numerous pen names, including Ki Magao, Kyōkadō, Shakujō Sanjin, Haikai Utagaba, Manyōtei, Shihō Utakaki, Shihō Magao, Kōyaō, and Renagawa Kōchō, among others.<ref name=":0">{{Cite|和書|author=[[Masaru Okamoto]] and [[Sumio Kumo]]|title=Shinpan Kinsei Bungaku Kenkyū Jiten|date=2006-02|pages=275|publisher=Ōfū|ref=harv}}</ref>
== Biography == In 1781 (Tenmei 1), Shikatsube's name first appear[[Category:pp to pp]]ed in the {{ill|sharebon|ja|洒落本}} *Sode Kagami* ("Sleeve Mirror"). During the Tenmei era, he published numerous works, including {{ill|kibyōshi|ja|黄表紙}} and sharebon.<ref name=":0" /> In 1782 (Tenmei 2), he entered the world of kyōka poetry under the tutelage of {{ill|Motoki Ami|ja|高嵩松}}. Around 1784 (Tenmei 4), he became a disciple of {{ill|Ōta Nanpo|ja|大田南畝}} and formed the "Sukiya Ren" group alongside {{ill|Sangi Arimasa|ja|算木有政}}, {{ill|Zenya Kanerachi|ja|銭屋金埒}}, and others.<ref name=":0" />
Eventually, he was counted among the "Four Heavenly Kings of Kyōka" alongside {{ill|Ishikawa Masamochi|ja|石川雅望}} (Yadoya no Meshimori), {{ill|Kishi Bunshō|ja|岸文笑}} (Atamimitsu), and Kanerachi.<ref name=":0" /> In 1794 (Kansei 6), Nanpo granted him the surname "Shihō," dividing the kyōka poetry world of the Kansei era with Yadoya no Meshimori.<ref name=":0" />
Magao aimed to correct what he saw as the unbridled and chaotic tendencies of Tenmei kyōka, advocating for {{ill|haikai uta|ja|俳諧歌}}, which he considered the original form of kyōka, rooted in Kamakura and Muromachi period traditions. However, this approach failed to gain public favor and ultimately contributed to the decline of kyōka.<ref name=":0" /> In his later years, he faced familial misfortune and poverty, dying in destitution.<ref name=":0" />

revision.comment: Bot: Reformatted page - moved categories, interwikis, DEFAULTSORT, and removed duplicates.
revision.text:
'''Shikatsube no Magao''' (鹿津部 真顔, 1753 (Hōreki 3) – July 6, 1829 (Bunsei 12, 6th day of the 6th month)) was a late Edo period {{ill|kyōka poet|ja|狂歌}} and {{ill|gesaku writer|ja|戯作者}}. His name is also written as 鹿都部真顔. He was commonly known as Kitagawa Kahei, with numerous pen names, including Ki Magao, Kyōkadō, Shakujō Sanjin, Haikai Utagaba, Manyōtei, Shihō Utakaki, Shihō Magao, Kōyaō, and Renagawa Kōchō, among others.<ref name=":0">{{Cite|和書|author=[[Masaru Okamoto]] and [[Sumio Kumo]]|title=Shinpan Kinsei Bungaku Kenkyū Jiten|date=2006-02|pages=275|publisher=Ōfū|ref=harv}}</ref>
== Biography == In 1781 (Tenmei 1), Shikatsube's name first appeared in the {{ill|sharebon|ja|洒落本}} *Sode Kagami* ("Sleeve Mirror"). During the Tenmei era, he published numerous works, including {{ill|kibyōshi|ja|黄表紙}} and sharebon.<ref name=":0" /> In 1782 (Tenmei 2), he entered the world of kyōka poetry under the tutelage of {{ill|Motoki Ami|ja|高嵩松}}. Around 1784 (Tenmei 4), he became a disciple of {{ill|Ōta Nanpo|ja|大田南畝}} and formed the "Sukiya Ren" group alongside {{ill|Sangi Arimasa|ja|算木有政}}, {{ill|Zenya Kanerachi|ja|銭屋金埒}}, and others.<ref name=":0" />
Eventually, he was counted among the "Four Heavenly Kings of Kyōka" alongside {{ill|Ishikawa Masamochi|ja|石川雅望}} (Yadoya no Meshimori), {{ill|Kishi Bunshō|ja|岸文笑}} (Atamimitsu), and Kanerachi.<ref name=":0" /> In 1794 (Kansei 6), Nanpo granted him the surname "Shihō," dividing the kyōka poetry world of the Kansei era with Yadoya no Meshimori.<ref name=":0" />
Magao aimed to correct what he saw as the unbridled and chaotic tendencies of Tenmei kyōka, advocating for {{ill|haikai uta|ja|俳諧歌}}, which he considered the original form of kyōka, rooted in Kamakura and Muromachi period traditions. However, this approach failed to gain public favor and ultimately contributed to the decline of kyōka.<ref name=":0" /> In his later years, he faced familial misfortune and poverty, dying in destitution.<ref name=":0" />
== References == {{Reflist}}{{Normdaten}}

He was commonly known as Kitagawa Kahei, with numerous pen names, including Ki Magao, Kyōkadō, Shakujō Sanjin, Haikai Utagaba, Manyōtei, Shihō Utakaki, Shihō Magao, Kōyaō, and Renagawa Kōchō, among others.<ref name=":0">{{Cite|和書|author=[[Masaru Okamoto]] and [[Sumio Kumo]]|title=Shinpan Kinsei Bungaku Kenkyū Jiten|date=2006-02|pages=275|publisher=Ōfū|ref=harv}}</ref>
== Biography == In 1781 (Tenmei 1), Shikatsube's name first appeared in the {{ill|sharebon|ja|洒落本|12=draft|12=draft}} *Sode Kagami* ("Sleeve Mirror"). During the Tenmei era, he published numerous works, including {{ill|kibyōshi|ja|黄表紙|12=draft|12=draft}} and sharebon.<ref name=":0" /> In 1782 (Tenmei 2), he entered the world of kyōka poetry under the tutelage of {{ill|Motoki Ami|ja|高嵩松|12=draft|12=draft}}. Around 1784 (Tenmei 4), he became a disciple of {{ill|Ōta Nanpo|ja|大田南畝|12=draft|12=draft}} and formed the "Sukiya Ren" group alongside {{ill|Sangi Arimasa|ja|算木有政|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Zenya Kanerachi|ja|銭屋金埒|12=draft|12=draft}}, and others.<ref name=":0" />
Eventually, he was counted among the "Four Heavenly Kings of Kyōka" alongside {{ill|Ishikawa Masamochi|ja|石川雅望|12=draft|12=draft}} (Yadoya no Meshimori), {{ill|Kishi Bunshō|ja|岸文笑|12=draft|12=draft}} (Atamimitsu), and Kanerachi.<ref name=":0" /> In 1794 (Kansei 6), Nanpo granted him the surname "Shihō," dividing the kyōka poetry world of the Kansei era with Yadoya no Meshimori.<ref name=":0" />
Magao aimed to correct what he saw as the unbridled and chaotic tendencies of Tenmei kyōka, advocating for {{ill|haikai uta|ja|俳諧歌|12=draft|12=draft}}, which he considered the original form of kyōka, rooted in Kamakura and Muromachi period traditions. However, this approach failed to gain public favor and ultimately contributed to the decline of kyōka.<ref name=":0" /> In his later years, he faced familial misfortune and poverty, dying in destitution.<ref name=":0" />

revision.text:
'''鹿津部 真顔'''（しかつべの まがお、[[1753年]]（[[宝暦]]3年） - [[1829年]][[7月6日]]（[[文政]]12年[[6月6日 (旧暦)|6月6日]]））は、[[江戸時代]]後期の[[狂歌師]]・[[戯作者]]。'''鹿都部真顔'''とも書く。通称は北川嘉兵衛、号に紀真顔・狂歌堂・鹿杖山人・俳諧歌場・万葉亭・四方歌垣・四方真顔・好屋翁・恋川好町など<ref name=":0">{{Cite|和書|author=[[岡本勝]]・[[雲英末雄]]|title=新版近世文学研究事典|date=2006-02|pages=275|publisher=おうふう|ref=harv}}</ref>。
== 略歴 == [[1781年]]（天明元年）洒落本『袖かがみ』に初めて名前が見え、[[黄表紙]]や[[洒落本]]などを天明年間に刊行する<ref name=":0" />。[[1782年]]（天明2年）に狂歌界へ入り、師は[[高嵩松|元木網]]で、[[1784年]]（天明4年）頃に[[大田南畝]]門下となり、[[算木有政]]や[[銭屋金埒]]らと「スキヤ連」を結成した<ref name=":0" />。やがて、[[石川雅望|宿屋飯盛]]・[[岸文笑|頭光]]・金埒らと狂歌四天王と称された<ref name=":0" />。[[1794年]]（[[寛政]]6年）南畝から「四方姓」を譲られ、飯盛と化成期の狂歌界を二分した<ref name=":0" />。真顔は天明狂歌が奔放無軌道に陥ったことを是正しようと、鎌倉室町期の狂歌を本来の姿であるとする「俳諧歌」を提唱したが、一般からは親しまれず、結果として狂歌の衰退をもたらした<ref name=":0" />。晩年は家庭的にも恵まれず、貧窮のうちに没した<ref name=":0" />。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}{{Normdaten}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:しかつへ の まかお}} [[Category:江戸時代の歌人]] [[Category:狂歌師]] [[Category:18世紀の歌人]] [[Category:19世紀の歌人]] [[Category:戯作者]] [[Category:武蔵国の人物]] [[Category:1753年生]] [[Category:1829年没]]

His name is also written as 鹿都部真顔. He was commonly known as Kitagawa Kahei, with numerous pen names, including Ki Magao, Kyōkadō, Shakujō Sanjin, Haikai Utagaba, Manyōtei, Shihō Utakaki, Shihō Magao, Kōyaō, and Renagawa Kōchō, among others.<ref name=":0">{{Cite|和書|author=[[Masaru Okamoto]] and [[Sumio Kumo]]|title=Shinpan Kinsei Bungaku Kenkyū Jiten|date=2006-02|pages=275|publisher=Ōfū|ref=harv}}</ref>

Around 1784 (Tenmei 4), he became a disciple of [[:en:Ōta Nanpo|Ōta Nanpo]] and formed the "Sukiya Ren" group alongside {{ill|Sangi Arimasa|ja|算木有政|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sangi Arimasa|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Zenya Kanerachi|ja|銭屋金埒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenya Kanerachi|qq=|1=Zenya Kanerachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98117575}}, and others.<ref name=":0" />

Magao aimed to correct what he saw as the unbridled and chaotic tendencies of Tenmei kyōka, advocating for {{ill|haikai uta|ja|俳諧歌|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/haikai uta|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which he considered the original form of kyōka, rooted in Kamakura and Muromachi period traditions. He was commonly known as Kitagawa Kahei, with numerous pen names, including Ki Magao, Kyōkadō, Shakujō Sanjin, Haikai Utagaba, Manyōtei, Shihō Utakaki, Shihō Magao, Kōyaō, and Renagawa Kōchō, among others.<ref name=":0">{{Cite|和書|author=[[Masaru Okamoto]] and [[Sumio Kumo]]|title=Shinpan Kinsei Bungaku Kenkyū Jiten|date=2006-02|pages=275|publisher=Ōfū|ref=harv}}</ref>
== Biography == In 1781 (Tenmei 1), Shikatsube's name first appeared in the [[:en:Sharebon|sharebon]] *Sode Kagami* ("Sleeve Mirror"). During the Tenmei era, he published numerous works, including [[:en:Kibyōshi|kibyōshi]] and sharebon.<ref name=":0" /> In 1782 (Tenmei 2), he entered the world of kyōka poetry under the tutelage of {{ill|Motoki Ami|ja|高嵩松|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Motoki Ami|qq=|1=Ko Susho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24865952}}. Around 1784 (Tenmei 4), he became a disciple of [[:en:Ōta Nanpo|Ōta Nanpo]] and formed the "Sukiya Ren" group alongside {{ill|Sangi Arimasa|ja|算木有政|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sangi Arimasa|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Zenya Kanerachi|ja|銭屋金埒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenya Kanerachi|qq=|1=Zenya Kanerachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98117575}}, and others.<ref name=":0" />
Eventually, he was counted among the "Four Heavenly Kings of Kyōka" alongside [[:en:Ishikawa Masamochi|Ishikawa Masamochi]] (Yadoya no Meshimori), {{ill|Kishi Bunshō|ja|岸文笑|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishi Bunshō|qq=|1=Kishi Bunshō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11475872}} (Atamimitsu), and Kanerachi.<ref name=":0" /> In 1794 (Kansei 6), Nanpo granted him the surname "Shihō," dividing the kyōka poetry world of the Kansei era with Yadoya no Meshimori.<ref name=":0" />
Magao aimed to correct what he saw as the unbridled and chaotic tendencies of Tenmei kyōka, advocating for {{ill|haikai uta|ja|俳諧歌|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/haikai uta|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which he considered the original form of kyōka, rooted in Kamakura and Muromachi period traditions. However, this approach failed to gain public favor and ultimately contributed to the decline of kyōka.<ref name=":0" /> In his later years, he faced familial misfortune and poverty, dying in destitution.<ref name=":0" />

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Yomo no Utagaki Magao]][[ja:鹿津部真顔]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1752; 1753|death_date=1829; {{death date|1829|7|6}}|birth_place={{ill|Sukiyabashi|WD=Q11499630}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|novelist|WD=Q6625963}}; {{ill|shopkeeper|WD=Q7501153}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11677231]] Wikidata name: Shikatsube no Magao [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11677231]] Wikidata name: Shikatsube no Magao [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:54, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shikatsube no Magao''' (鹿津部 真顔, 1753 (Hōreki 3) – July 6, 1829 (Bunsei 12, 6th day of the 6th month)) was a late Edo period [[:en:Kyōka|kyōka poet]] and {{ill|gesaku writer|ja|戯作者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gesaku writer|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 戯作|1=gesaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1047433}}. His name is also written as 鹿都部真顔. He was commonly known as Kitagawa Kahei, with numerous pen names, including Ki Magao, Kyōkadō, Shakujō Sanjin, Haikai Utagaba, Manyōtei, Shihō Utakaki, Shihō Magao, Kōyaō, and Renagawa Kōchō, among others.<ref name=":0">{{Cite|和書|author=[[Masaru Okamoto]] and [[Sumio Kumo]]|title=Shinpan Kinsei Bungaku Kenkyū Jiten|date=2006-02|pages=275|publisher=Ōfū|ref=harv}}</ref>
== Biography == In 1781 (Tenmei 1), Shikatsube's name first appeared in the [[:en:Sharebon|sharebon]] *Sode Kagami* ("Sleeve Mirror"). During the Tenmei era, he published numerous works, including [[:en:Kibyōshi|kibyōshi]] and sharebon.<ref name=":0" /> In 1782 (Tenmei 2), he entered the world of kyōka poetry under the tutelage of {{ill|Motoki Ami|ja|高嵩松|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Motoki Ami|1=Ko Susho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24865952}}. Around 1784 (Tenmei 4), he became a disciple of [[:en:Ōta Nanpo|Ōta Nanpo]] and formed the "Sukiya Ren" group alongside {{ill|Sangi Arimasa|ja|算木有政|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sangi Arimasa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Zenya Kanerachi|ja|銭屋金埒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenya Kanerachi|1=Zenya Kanerachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98117575}}, and others.<ref name=":0" />
Eventually, he was counted among the "Four Heavenly Kings of Kyōka" alongside [[:en:Ishikawa Masamochi|Ishikawa Masamochi]] (Yadoya no Meshimori), {{ill|Kishi Bunshō|ja|岸文笑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishi Bunshō|1=Kishi Bunshō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11475872}} (Atamimitsu), and Kanerachi.<ref name=":0" /> In 1794 (Kansei 6), Nanpo granted him the surname "Shihō," dividing the kyōka poetry world of the Kansei era with Yadoya no Meshimori.<ref name=":0" />
Magao aimed to correct what he saw as the unbridled and chaotic tendencies of Tenmei kyōka, advocating for {{ill|haikai uta|ja|俳諧歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/haikai uta|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which he considered the original form of kyōka, rooted in Kamakura and Muromachi period traditions. However, this approach failed to gain public favor and ultimately contributed to the decline of kyōka.<ref name=":0" /> In his later years, he faced familial misfortune and poverty, dying in destitution.<ref name=":0" />

Around 1784 (Tenmei 4), he became a disciple of [[:en:Ōta Nanpo|Ōta Nanpo]] and formed the "Sukiya Ren" group alongside {{ill|Sangi Arimasa|ja|算木有政|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sangi Arimasa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Zenya Kanerachi|ja|銭屋金埒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenya Kanerachi|1=Zenya Kanerachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98117575}}, and others.<ref name=":0" />
Eventually, he was counted among the "Four Heavenly Kings of Kyōka" alongside [[:en:Ishikawa Masamochi|Ishikawa Masamochi]] (Yadoya no Meshimori), {{ill|Kishi Bunshō|ja|岸文笑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishi Bunshō|1=Kishi Bunshō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11475872}} (Atamimitsu), and Kanerachi.<ref name=":0" /> In 1794 (Kansei 6), Nanpo granted him the surname "Shihō," dividing the kyōka poetry world of the Kansei era with Yadoya no Meshimori.<ref name=":0" />
Magao aimed to correct what he saw as the unbridled and chaotic tendencies of Tenmei kyōka, advocating for {{ill|haikai uta|ja|俳諧歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/haikai uta|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which he considered the original form of kyōka, rooted in Kamakura and Muromachi period traditions. However, this approach failed to gain public favor and ultimately contributed to the decline of kyōka.<ref name=":0" /> In his later years, he faced familial misfortune and poverty, dying in destitution.<ref name=":0" />

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Yomo no Utagaki Magao]][[ja:鹿津部真顔]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1752; 1753|death_date=1829; {{death date|1829|7|6}}|birth_place={{ill|Sukiyabashi|WD=Q11499630}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|novelist|WD=Q6625963}}; {{ill|shopkeeper|WD=Q7501153}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11677231]] Wikidata name: Shikatsube no Magao [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11677231]] Wikidata name: Shikatsube no Magao [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:54, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shikatsube no Magao''' (鹿津部 真顔, 1753 (Hōreki 3) – July 6, 1829 (Bunsei 12, 6th day of the 6th month)) was a late Edo period [[:en:Kyōka|kyōka poet]] and {{ill|gesaku writer|ja|戯作者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gesaku writer|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 戯作|1=gesaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1047433}}. His name is also written as 鹿都部真顔. He was commonly known as Kitagawa Kahei, with numerous pen names, including Ki Magao, Kyōkadō, Shakujō Sanjin, Haikai Utagaba, Manyōtei, Shihō Utakaki, Shihō Magao, Kōyaō, and Renagawa Kōchō, among others.<ref name=":0">{{Cite|和書|author=[[Masaru Okamoto]] and [[Sumio Kumo]]|title=Shinpan Kinsei Bungaku Kenkyū Jiten|date=2006-02|pages=275|publisher=Ōfū|ref=harv}}</ref>
== Biography == In 1781 (Tenmei 1), Shikatsube's name first appeared in the [[:en:Sharebon|sharebon]] *Sode Kagami* ("Sleeve Mirror"). During the Tenmei era, he published numerous works, including [[:en:Kibyōshi|kibyōshi]] and sharebon.<ref name=":0" /> In 1782 (Tenmei 2), he entered the world of kyōka poetry under the tutelage of {{ill|Motoki Ami|ja|高嵩松|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Motoki Ami|1=Ko Susho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24865952}}. Around 1784 (Tenmei 4), he became a disciple of [[:en:Ōta Nanpo|Ōta Nanpo]] and formed the "Sukiya Ren" group alongside {{ill|Sangi Arimasa|ja|算木有政|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sangi Arimasa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Zenya Kanerachi|ja|銭屋金埒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenya Kanerachi|1=Zenya Kanerachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98117575}}, and others.<ref name=":0" />
Eventually, he was counted among the "Four Heavenly Kings of Kyōka" alongside [[:en:Ishikawa Masamochi|Ishikawa Masamochi]] (Yadoya no Meshimori), {{ill|Kishi Bunshō|ja|岸文笑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishi Bunshō|1=Kishi Bunshō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11475872}} (Atamimitsu), and Kanerachi.<ref name=":0" /> In 1794 (Kansei 6), Nanpo granted him the surname "Shihō," dividing the kyōka poetry world of the Kansei era with Yadoya no Meshimori.<ref name=":0" />
Magao aimed to correct what he saw as the unbridled and chaotic tendencies of Tenmei kyōka, advocating for {{ill|haikai uta|ja|俳諧歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/haikai uta|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which he considered the original form of kyōka, rooted in Kamakura and Muromachi period traditions. However, this approach failed to gain public favor and ultimately contributed to the decline of kyōka.<ref name=":0" /> In his later years, he faced familial misfortune and poverty, dying in destitution.<ref name=":0" />

However, this approach failed to gain public favor and ultimately contributed to the decline of kyōka.<ref name=":0" /> In his later years, he faced familial misfortune and poverty, dying in destitution.<ref name=":0" />

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Yomo no Utagaki Magao]][[ja:鹿津部真顔]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Yomo no Utagaki Magao]][[ja:鹿津部真顔]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Yomo no Utagaki Magao]][[ja:鹿津部真顔]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Yomo no Utagaki Magao]][[ja:鹿津部真顔]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1752; 1753|death_date=1829; {{death date|1829|7|6}}|birth_place={{ill|Sukiyabashi|WD=Q11499630}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|novelist|WD=Q6625963}}; {{ill|shopkeeper|WD=Q7501153}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11677231]] Wikidata name: Shikatsube no Magao [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11677231]] Wikidata name: Shikatsube no Magao [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:54, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shikatsube no Magao''' (鹿津部 真顔, 1753 (Hōreki 3) – July 6, 1829 (Bunsei 12, 6th day of the 6th month)) was a late Edo period [[:en:Kyōka|kyōka poet]] and {{ill|gesaku writer|ja|戯作者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gesaku writer|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 戯作|1=gesaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1047433}}. His name is also written as 鹿都部真顔. He was commonly known as Kitagawa Kahei, with numerous pen names, including Ki Magao, Kyōkadō, Shakujō Sanjin, Haikai Utagaba, Manyōtei, Shihō Utakaki, Shihō Magao, Kōyaō, and Renagawa Kōchō, among others.<ref name=":0">{{Cite|和書|author=[[Masaru Okamoto]] and [[Sumio Kumo]]|title=Shinpan Kinsei Bungaku Kenkyū Jiten|date=2006-02|pages=275|publisher=Ōfū|ref=harv}}</ref>
== Biography == In 1781 (Tenmei 1), Shikatsube's name first appeared in the [[:en:Sharebon|sharebon]] *Sode Kagami* ("Sleeve Mirror"). During the Tenmei era, he published numerous works, including [[:en:Kibyōshi|kibyōshi]] and sharebon.<ref name=":0" /> In 1782 (Tenmei 2), he entered the world of kyōka poetry under the tutelage of {{ill|Motoki Ami|ja|高嵩松|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Motoki Ami|1=Ko Susho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24865952}}. Around 1784 (Tenmei 4), he became a disciple of [[:en:Ōta Nanpo|Ōta Nanpo]] and formed the "Sukiya Ren" group alongside {{ill|Sangi Arimasa|ja|算木有政|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sangi Arimasa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Zenya Kanerachi|ja|銭屋金埒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenya Kanerachi|1=Zenya Kanerachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98117575}}, and others.<ref name=":0" />
Eventually, he was counted among the "Four Heavenly Kings of Kyōka" alongside [[:en:Ishikawa Masamochi|Ishikawa Masamochi]] (Yadoya no Meshimori), {{ill|Kishi Bunshō|ja|岸文笑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishi Bunshō|1=Kishi Bunshō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11475872}} (Atamimitsu), and Kanerachi.<ref name=":0" /> In 1794 (Kansei 6), Nanpo granted him the surname "Shihō," dividing the kyōka poetry world of the Kansei era with Yadoya no Meshimori.<ref name=":0" />
Magao aimed to correct what he saw as the unbridled and chaotic tendencies of Tenmei kyōka, advocating for {{ill|haikai uta|ja|俳諧歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/haikai uta|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which he considered the original form of kyōka, rooted in Kamakura and Muromachi period traditions. However, this approach failed to gain public favor and ultimately contributed to the decline of kyōka.<ref name=":0" /> In his later years, he faced familial misfortune and poverty, dying in destitution.<ref name=":0" />

Magao aimed to correct what he saw as the unbridled and chaotic tendencies of Tenmei kyōka, advocating for {{ill|haikai uta|ja|俳諧歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/haikai uta|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which he considered the original form of kyōka, rooted in Kamakura and Muromachi period traditions. However, this approach failed to gain public favor and ultimately contributed to the decline of kyōka.<ref name=":0" /> In his later years, he faced familial misfortune and poverty, dying in destitution.<ref name=":0" />

He was commonly known as Kitagawa Kahei, with numerous pen names, including Ki Magao, Kyōkadō, Shakujō Sanjin, Haikai Utagaba, Manyōtei, Shihō Utakaki, Shihō Magao, Kōyaō, and Renagawa Kōchō, among others.<ref name=":0">{{Cite|和書|author=[[Masaru Okamoto]] and [[Sumio Kumo]]|title=Shinpan Kinsei Bungaku Kenkyū Jiten|date=2006-02|pages=275|publisher=Ōfū|ref=harv}}</ref>
== Biography == In 1781 (Tenmei 1), Shikatsube's name first appeared in the {{ill|Sharebon|en|Sharebon|WD=Q3481480|lt=sharebon}} *Sode Kagami* ("Sleeve Mirror"). During the Tenmei era, he published numerous works, including {{ill|Kibyōshi|en|Kibyōshi|WD=Q3196335|lt=kibyōshi}} and sharebon.<ref name=":0" /> In 1782 (Tenmei 2), he entered the world of kyōka poetry under the tutelage of {{ill|Motoki Ami|ja|高嵩松|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Motoki Ami|1=Ko Susho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24865952}}. Around 1784 (Tenmei 4), he became a disciple of {{ill|Ōta Nanpo|en|Ōta Nanpo|WD=Q386183|lt=Ōta Nanpo}} and formed the "Sukiya Ren" group alongside {{ill|Sangi Arimasa|ja|算木有政|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sangi Arimasa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Zenya Kanerachi|ja|銭屋金埒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenya Kanerachi|1=Zenya Kanerachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98117575}}, and others.<ref name=":0" />
Eventually, he was counted among the "Four Heavenly Kings of Kyōka" alongside {{ill|Ishikawa Masamochi|en|Ishikawa Masamochi|WD=Q6535184|lt=Ishikawa Masamochi}} (Yadoya no Meshimori), {{ill|Kishi Bunshō|ja|岸文笑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishi Bunshō|1=Kishi Bunshō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11475872}} (Atamimitsu), and Kanerachi.<ref name=":0" /> In 1794 (Kansei 6), Nanpo granted him the surname "Shihō," dividing the kyōka poetry world of the Kansei era with Yadoya no Meshimori.<ref name=":0" />
Magao aimed to correct what he saw as the unbridled and chaotic tendencies of Tenmei kyōka, advocating for {{ill|haikai uta|ja|俳諧歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/haikai uta|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which he considered the original form of kyōka, rooted in Kamakura and Muromachi period traditions. However, this approach failed to gain public favor and ultimately contributed to the decline of kyōka.<ref name=":0" /> In his later years, he faced familial misfortune and poverty, dying in destitution.<ref name=":0" />

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Yomo no Utagaki Magao]][[ja:鹿津部真顔]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Yomo no Utagaki Magao]][[ja:鹿津部真顔]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Yomo no Utagaki Magao]][[ja:鹿津部真顔]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Yomo no Utagaki Magao]][[ja:鹿津部真顔]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1752; 1753|death_date=1829; {{death date|1829|7|6}}|birth_place={{ill|Sukiyabashi|WD=Q11499630|1=Sukiyabashi}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17|1=Japan}}|occupation={{ill|novelist|WD=Q6625963|1=Novelist}}; {{ill|shopkeeper|WD=Q7501153|1=Shopkeeper}}}}
'''Shikatsube no Magao''' (鹿津部 真顔, 1753 (Hōreki 3) – July 6, 1829 (Bunsei 12, 6th day of the 6th month)) was a late Edo period {{ill|Kyōka|en|Kyōka|WD=Q3201017|lt=kyōka poet|1=Kyōka}} and {{ill|gesaku writer|ja|戯作者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gesaku writer|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 戯作|1=Gesaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1047433}}. His name is also written as 鹿都部真顔. He was commonly known as Kitagawa Kahei, with numerous pen names, including Ki Magao, Kyōkadō, Shakujō Sanjin, Haikai Utagaba, Manyōtei, Shihō Utakaki, Shihō Magao, Kōyaō, and Renagawa Kōchō, among others.<ref name=":0">{{Cite|和書|author=[[Masaru Okamoto]] and [[Sumio Kumo]]|title=Shinpan Kinsei Bungaku Kenkyū Jiten|date=2006-02|pages=275|publisher=Ōfū|ref=harv}}</ref>
== Biography == In 1781 (Tenmei 1), Shikatsube's name first appeared in the {{ill|Sharebon|en|Sharebon|WD=Q3481480|lt=sharebon|1=Sharebon}} *Sode Kagami* ("Sleeve Mirror"). During the Tenmei era, he published numerous works, including {{ill|Kibyōshi|en|Kibyōshi|WD=Q3196335|lt=kibyōshi|1=Kibyōshi}} and sharebon.<ref name=":0" /> In 1782 (Tenmei 2), he entered the world of kyōka poetry under the tutelage of {{ill|Motoki Ami|ja|高嵩松|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Motoki Ami|1=Ko Susho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24865952}}. Around 1784 (Tenmei 4), he became a disciple of {{ill|Ōta Nanpo|en|Ōta Nanpo|WD=Q386183|lt=Ōta Nanpo|1=Ōta Nanpo}} and formed the "Sukiya Ren" group alongside {{ill|Sangi Arimasa|ja|算木有政|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sangi Arimasa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Zenya Kanerachi|ja|銭屋金埒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenya Kanerachi|1=Q98117575|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q98117575}}, and others.<ref name=":0" />
Eventually, he was counted among the "Four Heavenly Kings of Kyōka" alongside {{ill|Ishikawa Masamochi|en|Ishikawa Masamochi|WD=Q6535184|lt=Ishikawa Masamochi|1=Ishikawa Masamochi}} (Yadoya no Meshimori), {{ill|Kishi Bunshō|ja|岸文笑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishi Bunshō|1=Kishi Bunshō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11475872}} (Atamimitsu), and Kanerachi.<ref name=":0" /> In 1794 (Kansei 6), Nanpo granted him the surname "Shihō," dividing the kyōka poetry world of the Kansei era with Yadoya no Meshimori.<ref name=":0" />
Magao aimed to correct what he saw as the unbridled and chaotic tendencies of Tenmei kyōka, advocating for {{ill|haikai uta|ja|俳諧歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/haikai uta|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which he considered the original form of kyōka, rooted in Kamakura and Muromachi period traditions. However, this approach failed to gain public favor and ultimately contributed to the decline of kyōka.<ref name=":0" /> In his later years, he faced familial misfortune and poverty, dying in destitution.<ref name=":0" />
